summary: O'Neil argues against embedding algorithms directly into laws, warning that this would add mathematical opacity to an already opaque legal system. She notes that lawmakers' incentives often aren't aligned with algorithms 'working better,' and that lobbyists would exploit black-box algorithms to further obfuscate regulations. While algorithms may be useful as tools for regulatory agencies like the SEC to prioritize cases, even this application risks encoding bias, as seen in Stop & Frisk policies that disproportionately target minorities. She concludes that the combination of algorithmic opacity and misaligned incentives makes direct embedding in law dangerous.
key_points: Adding algorithmic black boxes to law would compound existing opacity problems and be exploited by lobbyists who already write much financial regulation
The secrecy argument for algorithms is flawed—if people can game your model, your proxies aren't truly behavior-based
Laws must be understandable by those expected to follow them; requiring a PhD and Python knowledge to understand regulations violates this principle
Algorithms as regulatory tools (like SEC fraud detection) are reasonable, but create workflow rankings rather than law itself
Predictive policing algorithms encode and amplify existing biases, resulting in poor communities being over-policed while wealthy areas go unchecked
money_quotes: If you gave them a new tool, that of adding black box algorithms, then you could be sure they would use it to further obfuscate what is already a hopelessly complicated set of rules.
If people can game your model, that just means your model sucks, and specifically that your proxies are not truly behavior-based.
Anyone who is under the expectation of following a law should also be able to read and understand the law.
It wouldn't be enough to have the source code available, since that's only accessible to a very small subset of the population.

If you live in a nice neighborhood, the limited police force and inspection agencies never check you out since their algorithms have decided the probability of bad stuff happening is too low to bother.
It kind of makes sense that white kids smoke pot at the same rate as black kids but are almost never busted for it.
key_insight: Algorithmic opacity combined with misaligned incentives makes embedding algorithms in law dangerous, as they will inevitably be exploited by powerful interests and encode existing societal biases.
